FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study data (NIMH Data Archive, release 4.0, DOI 10.15154/1,523,041), 21 recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10 explicitly stated.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: exploratory factor analysis to form risk-score composite, linear mixed-effects regression comparing risk groups on behavioral and fMRI outcomes, Spearman correlations between neural and impulsivity measures.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (Vol 76, 2025, pp. 690-701); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate appears in any results table or figure where a phone/SM-specific variable (screen time alone) is paired with an individual-trait outcome. Screen time (Factor 5: weekday + weekend screen time) is one ingredient in a six-factor composite risk score that also includes SES, parental behavior (aggressiveness, ADHD), nutrition (fat/sugar intake), family environment (conflict), and prenatal exposure (caffeine/alcohol). All reported betas and group differences are for risk group (low/medium/high, defined by the composite) vs. behavioral/neural outcomes, not for screen time variable independently. Step 2: Screen time is never analyzed as a standalone variable; it is embedded in a multi-factor composite containing non-phone/non-SM items (nutrition, family violence, parental psychopathology, prenatal exposure, SES). The composite fails Step 2 because it bundles non-phone/non-SM ingredients without separate breakout for screen time.

notes: Screen time is Factor 5 in a six-factor composite risk score. The main analyses compare low/medium/high-risk groups (defined by the entire composite) on fMRI, cortical thickness, BAS, UPPS-P, and CBCL outcomes. No analysis isolates screen time as IV with an individual-trait DV. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, prospective cohort, 21 U.S. sites, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-11, ABCD Study explicitly named.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Cox proportional hazards models examining prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with BMI percentile transition from overweight/obesity to normal.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Obesity (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only screen time variable analyzed is 'Total screen time' (continuous and categorical). No phone/SM-specific labeled variable (e.g., social media time, texting, video chat, named platform) appears in any results table or sensitivity analysis. Step 2: Per Methods, the screen time composite is the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey total computed as weighted average across 'watching/streaming shows, TV, movies, watching/streaming videos (e.g., YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, FaceTime), and using social media (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter).' This composite bundles TV/movies, video games (console/non-phone unspecified), alongside social media and texting. It contains disqualifying non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV/movies, video games unspecified platform) without a per-modality breakout anywhere in the paper. Fails Step 2.

notes: Screen time composite (ABCD YSTS total) includes TV, video games, video chat, social media, texting as a single aggregate. No per-modality breakout is reported in main or supplementary tables. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 7445 participants aged 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), 21 U.S. sites explicitly named.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective longitudinal linear regression models estimating independent and joint associations of screen time and sleep parameters with BMI and waist circumference at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Childhood Obesity (October 2025, Vol 21, No 7, pp. 618-628); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The Methods state screen time was categorized by type: TV shows/movies, video games, and socializing (texting, video chatting, and social media), and that Supplementary Tables S6 and S7 show associations of specific screen time modalities with BMI and WC. The socializing subtype (texting + video chatting + social media) is a phone/SM-specific composite exclusively of qualifying items. The paper reports these modality-specific analyses 'with results consistent with the main findings.' BMI and waist circumference are physical health outcomes = individual-trait-level. A qualifying phone/SM-specific estimate (socializing modality coefficient with BMI/WC) exists in the supplement. FT-IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (B) with 95% CI and p-values are reported for screen time vs. BMI (B = 0.32; 95% CI: 0.19, 0.45; p < 0.001) and WC (B = 0.40; 95% CI: 0.23, 0.56; p < 0.001) in main Tables 2 and 3, and modality-specific results in Supplementary Tables S6-S7.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis confirmed: weighted multivariable linear regression with quantitative results (beta coefficients, 95% CI, p-values) for screen time-BMI and screen time-WC associations.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the IV (predictor) and BMI/waist circumference are the DVs in all analyses. FT-EC7 requires SM/screen time to be the DV in every analysis; here it is consistently the IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: INCLUDE. Screen time (including socializing subtype = texting + video chat + social media) as IV; BMI and waist circumference as outcomes. Prospective longitudinal design, baseline 2016-2018, 2-year follow-up. Modality-specific analyses in Supplementary Tables S6-S7 include the qualifying socializing category.
